title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
summary:
Henry Nigges informs Poirot that his wife believes their village is cursed, a sentiment stemming from Richard Nigges's sudden desire to leave the village and break off his engagement. Henry reveals that a few months before Richard left, he wrote a letter stating that the local young vicar and his wife had died within hours of each other in a "terrible tragedy," though the specific cause of their deaths was never explained.

While walking through London, Poirot and Kitcher discuss whether these past events are connected to the recent triple murder at the Brooks Han Hotel. Poirot posits that the tragic deaths of the clergy couple sixteen years ago triggered a chain of events: Richard's aversion to the church and the Bible, his decision to flee to Devonshire, his subsequent life of debauchery, and his eventual death alongside Ada Gransbury and Harriet Sipper. Poirot notes that Richard's mental state had recently improved before he was found dead with an engraved cufflink in his mouth.

Kitcher argues that Richard's loss of faith might be a personal crisis unrelated to the priest's death, using a crossword puzzle analogy to suggest multiple plausible explanations exist for any given event. Poirot rejects this, insisting that the victims were real people rather than mere clues. He instructs Kitcher to travel to the village of Glebeholme to investigate the true identities of the victims and the cause of the clergy couple's deaths. Kitcher is also tasked with obtaining guest and staff lists from the Bluxton Hall Hotel to check for connections to the village, as well as investigating the identities of "Jane" and "PIJ."
